title: Data leadership | Stats NZ
content:
The Government Chief Data Steward (GCDS) role supports the use of data as a resource across government to help deliver better services to New Zealanders. It is held by the Chief Executive of Stats NZ, Colin Lynch.
Government Chief Data Steward (GCDS) provides more information about the role.
Stats NZ is the lead agency for government-held data.
What we want to achieve Government Data Strategy and Roadmap NZ Government data system map Algorithm charter for Aotearoa New Zealand Analytics and research in government (ARG) internship and graduate programme Data Investment Plan Data Maturity Assessment Data standards Partnering with Māori Advisory and governance groups Data Ethics Advisory Group Information Group Mandated Standards Implementation and Review Committee Data frameworks Data Capability Framework Data Investment Framework Data Stewardship Framework Operational Data Governance Framework Ngā Tikanga Paihere Data tools Data governance Data management Data storage Introduction to data Open data Policy Principles for safe and effective use of data and analytics Legislative change Contact us

## What we want to achieve
Our goal is to empower government agencies to get value from data.
We’re partnering with agencies to:
- increase their capability to manage and use data
- identify and remove roadblocks to accessing data
- implement data standards and algorithm charter
- use new methodologies.
We’re also working with iwi and Māori to make sure they can take part in decisions about their data. At the heart of this work is transparency, trust, and integrity around the use of data the government holds.

## Government Data Strategy and Roadmap
The refreshed Government Data Strategy and Roadmap was published by the Government Chief Data Steward in September 2021. It provides a shared direction and plan for the government data system of Aotearoa NZ. The four focus areas of the system strategy are data, capability, infrastructure, and leadership. Each initiative in the Roadmap falls under one or more of these focus areas. Underpinning everything is the government’s responsibility to uphold Te Tiriti and a commitment to maintain and enhance public trust and confidence.
The Government Data Strategy and Roadmap provides more information.

## NZ Government data system map
The New Zealand Government data system map shows the flow of data between Kiwis, government, and organisations, and the checks and balances that are in place to help change lives and transform how we connect with the world.
NZ Government data system map

## Algorithm charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
Algorithms have an essential role to play in supporting the delivery of government services to people in New Zealand. The Algorithm charter for Aotearoa New Zealand is a commitment by government agencies to use algorithms in a fair, ethical, and transparent way.
Algorithm charter for Aotearoa New Zealand Algorithm charter community of practice

## Analytics and research in government (AR) internship and graduate programmes
The ARG programmes are multi-agency initiatives to build capability in data, analytics and research across government. Stats NZ are the lead agency for running these programmes.
Analytics and research in government

## Data Investment Plan
The Data Investment Plan is a prioritised plan to guide government investment in data.
The Plan will support a more co-ordinated and systematic approach to data investment by:
- helping agencies to make baseline-funding investment decisions about existing assets
- helping agencies to identify opportunities to develop joint Budget bids
- supporting Ministers and system leads (Treasury, Government Chief Data Steward, Government Chief Digital Officer) to better understand the data asset investment environment.
The Data Investment Plan 2022

## Data Maturity Assessment
The Data Maturity Assessment offers a comprehensive and actionable assessment of data practice capabilities in an organisation. It does this by measuring data practice based on levels of maturity.
Data Maturity Assessment
Agreed standards ensure information is recorded in a consistent way across agencies. It means there’s only one way of talking about each different piece of information.
Data standards provides more information.

## Partnering with Māori
We’re collaborating with iwi and community groups on real-world issues. We’ll use the lessons learned to develop relevant and innovative products and services. Co-designing Māori data governance provides more information.

## Advisory and governance groups

### Data Ethics Advisory Group
The Data Ethics Advisory Group is convened by the GCDS to help maximise the opportunities and benefits from new and emerging uses of data, while responsibly managing potential risks and harms.
Data Ethics Advisory Group
The Information Group advises from a system-wide perspective on how government can better unlock the value of the information it holds on behalf of citizens. It is chaired by the Deputy Statistician and Deputy Chief Executive Data System Leadership, Stats NZ.

### Mandated Standards Implementation and Review Committee
The Mandated standards implementation and review committee (IRC) is a cross-agency group made up of public sector representative members. The IRC advises on whether a standard should be mandated for use by Public Service Departments and Departmental Agencies.
Mandated Standards Implementation and Review Committee

### Data Capability Framework
We have built a data capability framework to empower agencies to assess and uplift the data capabilities of their people.
Data capability framework

### Data Investment Framework
Over recent years, ministers and officials have sought the GCDS’s view on government investment in data. The Data Investment Framework was created to guide the way investment in the data system could be made to deliver to government priorities, while also building the foundations required to ensure our data system can meet future needs.
The Data Investment Framework

### Data Stewardship Framework
We have designed a Data Stewardship Framework to enable agencies to better manage and safely share New Zealand’s data. The framework provides a structure and common language for organising and describing the different elements required for effective data stewardship.
A data stewardship framework for NZ

### Operation Data Governance Framework
Effective data stewardship depends on good data governance. Stats NZ partnered across government to design a new operational Data Governance Framework (oDGF) based on the principles of knowledge and care.
Operational data governance provides more information.
Ngā Tikanga Paihere draws on 10 tikanga (Te Ao Māori - Māori world concepts) to help you establish goals, boundaries, and principles that guide and inform your data practice.
Data governance is a system of decision rights and accountabilities which describe who can take what actions with what information, and when, under what circumstances, using what methods. Good governance ensures data availability, quality, security, usability, and integrity.
Guidance and resources related to the effective management of data.
The way your data is stored can affect how easy it is to access, use, and keep secure.

### Introduction to data
What is data and why it is important.
Introduction to data
We’re supporting agencies to proactively release open data. Open government data is non-personal government-held data that’s easy to access and use.
Open data provides more information.
The policies, principles, and frameworks guiding the way agencies manage and use data. Policies The policies, principles, and frameworks guiding the way agencies manage and use data.

### Principles for safe and effective use of data and analytics
The GCDS and the Privacy Commissioner have jointly developed six key principles to support safe and effective data analytics, including algorithmic decision-making.
Principles for safe and effective use of data and analytics
Detailed guidance and advice is being developed to support government agencies to take a best-practice approach to data analytics activities.
Privacy, security, and confidentiality provides more information.
The Data and Statistics Act 2022 came into force on 1 September 2022. This Act repeals and replaces the Statistics Act 1975.
If you have questions or feedback, please email datalead@stats.govt.nz
